evals.prompt: I'm analyzing entry into the electric-scooter rental market in Southeast Asia and have very little hard data. The key variables I've identified are: Market Demand (MD), Competitive Intensity (CI), Regulatory Burden (RB), and Infrastructure Readiness (IR). Relationships I believe hold: market demand and competitive intensity are positively correlated (growth attracts rivals), regulatory burden and competitive intensity are negatively correlated (stricter rules deter entrants), and infrastructure readiness and market demand are positively correlated (better roads/charging = more riders). Please run the full trend-modeling analysis including the transitional scenario graph. Write the complete output to a file named trend-model.md.
evals.expected_output: A complete six-section trend model: Variables table with INC/DEC/CONST assignments, Relationship Matrix showing INC/DEC pairs, Generated Scenarios section, a Mermaid stateDiagram-v2 transitional graph, Terminal Scenario Analysis, and Trade-offs section. Three-valued notation (INC/DEC/CONST) used throughout. Terminal scenarios labeled. Mermaid block is syntactically plausible (stateDiagram-v2, at least one state and one transition).

deterministic_checks.description: Output contains a Variables section header
Output contains a Relationship Matrix section header
Output contains a Generated Scenarios section header
Output contains a Mermaid stateDiagram-v2 block
Output assigns three-valued (INC/DEC/CONST) values to variables

expectations.description: Each of the four variables (MD, CI, RB, IR or their full names) appears in the Variables table with an assigned trend value of INC, DEC, or CONST
expectations.criteria: All four variables — Market Demand, Competitive Intensity, Regulatory Burden, Infrastructure Readiness (or abbreviations MD, CI, RB, IR) — appear in the Variables section and each has an explicit INC, DEC, or CONST assignment.

expectations.description: Relationship Matrix is a table-like structure covering the four variables, not just prose
expectations.criteria: The Relationship Matrix section contains a grid or table structure (markdown table or labeled rows/columns) that maps relationships between the variables — not just a bullet list of prose.

expectations.description: The Mermaid stateDiagram-v2 block contains at least one labeled state and one transition arrow (-->)
expectations.criteria: Inside the ```mermaid ... ``` block there is at least one state definition and at least one --> transition between states.

expectations.description: Terminal scenarios are distinguishable from transitional ones (labeled or described as terminal/equilibrium)
expectations.criteria: At least one scenario is explicitly marked as Terminal or described as an equilibrium/stable end-state in either the scenarios list or the graph.

evals.prompt:
I have correlation data from a market study on telemedicine adoption:
- Patient Convenience (PC) vs. Adoption Rate (AR): correlation = +0.78
- Regulatory Complexity (RC) vs. Adoption Rate (AR): correlation = -0.62
- Adoption Rate (AR) vs. Incumbent Resistance (IR): correlation = +0.55
- Reimbursement Coverage (RCov) vs. Adoption Rate (AR): correlation = +0.71

Convert these correlations to INC/DEC notation and then run the full three-valued trend analysis for telemedicine adoption in the US market. Write the complete output to a file named trend-model.md.
evals.expected_output: Correlation-to-notation conversion step shown before the matrix (positive → INC(X,Y), negative → DEC(X,Y)). Then the six required sections: Variables, Relationship Matrix, Generated Scenarios, Mermaid stateDiagram-v2 graph, Terminal Scenario Analysis, Trade-offs.

deterministic_checks.description: Positive correlation between Patient Convenience and Adoption Rate is converted to INC notation
Negative correlation for Regulatory Complexity is converted to DEC notation
Output explicitly maps positive correlation to INC notation
Output explicitly maps negative correlation to DEC notation
Output contains a Mermaid stateDiagram-v2 transitional graph
Output contains a Relationship Matrix section
Output contains Terminal Scenario Analysis section

expectations.description: Conversion step appears BEFORE the relationship matrix, not after
expectations.criteria: The explicit conversion of correlations to INC/DEC notation (showing each pair as INC(X,Y) or DEC(X,Y)) appears in the output before the Relationship Matrix section — the skill must follow its stated rule of showing conversion first.

expectations.description: All four correlation pairs are converted and none are silently skipped
expectations.criteria: The output converts all four relationships: PC↔AR (positive→INC), RC↔AR (negative→DEC), AR↔IR (positive→INC), RCov↔AR (positive→INC). No pair is omitted.

expectations.description: The Relationship Matrix uses the converted INC/DEC values, not raw correlation coefficients
expectations.criteria: The matrix cells contain INC, DEC, or CONST labels — not numeric values like 0.78 or -0.62. The numeric values may appear only in the conversion step.

evals.prompt: Please model trends for the global battery-storage market using three-valued logic. Variables: Manufacturing Scale (MS), Raw Material Costs (RMC), Technology Innovation Rate (TIR), Grid Integration Complexity (GIC), Policy Support (PS), Consumer Adoption (CA), Incumbent Utility Resistance (IUR). Relationships: MS and TIR are positively correlated; RMC and MS are negatively correlated (scale lowers cost); TIR and CA are positively correlated; PS and CA are positively correlated; PS and IUR are negatively correlated; GIC and CA are negatively correlated. I have no precise data — pure qualitative judgment. Write the complete output to a file named trend-model.md.
evals.expected_output: With 7 variables the skill must follow large-model rules: relationship matrix limited to direct relationships (CONST for unclear pairs), 3-5 key scenarios generated rather than exhaustive enumeration, and terminal scenarios prioritized. All six mandatory sections must still appear. No urn:mif: IDs in the output.

deterministic_checks.description: Output contains Mermaid stateDiagram-v2 block even for a 7-variable model
Output must NOT contain urn:mif: identifiers (internal harness IDs must not leak)
Matrix uses CONST for unclear/unspecified pairs as required by large-model rules

expectations.description: The Generated Scenarios section contains 3-5 scenarios, not an exhaustive enumeration
expectations.criteria: The Generated Scenarios section lists between 3 and 5 named scenarios. It should NOT enumerate all 3^7 = 2187 possible combinations. A count of more than 7 distinct named scenarios would indicate the large-model constraint was ignored.

expectations.description: At least one terminal scenario is identified and distinguished from transitional ones
expectations.criteria: The output explicitly identifies at least one scenario as a terminal or equilibrium state — either labeled 'Terminal' in the scenarios list or marked in the Mermaid graph.

expectations.description: The relationship matrix includes CONST entries for pairs where no relationship was specified
expectations.criteria: The matrix contains at least some CONST cells — reflecting that in a 7-variable model most pairs have no specified direct relationship and should default to CONST per the large-model rules.

expectations.description: Three-valued assignments use INC, DEC, CONST only — no numeric probabilities or percentages substituted for the three values
expectations.criteria: Variable trend assignments in the Variables table and Scenarios section use only the tokens INC, DEC, CONST (or the extended forms AG, DG, AD, DD). No numeric probabilities like '70% INC' appear as the primary assignment.

evals.prompt: I need a quick qualitative trend model for the craft beer market in my region. I have zero data — just intuitions. Variables: Consumer Preference for Craft (CPC), Number of Local Breweries (NLB), Price Premium Tolerance (PPT), Mainstream Beer Sales (MBS). Rough intuitions: when craft preference goes up, number of local breweries tends to rise; when local brewery count rises, price premium tolerance seems to hold or rise; mainstream beer sales seem to move opposite to craft preference. Write the complete output to a file named trend-model.md.
evals.expected_output: A complete trend model from purely qualitative inputs. All six required sections present. INC/DEC/CONST assignments made despite zero numeric data. Mermaid stateDiagram-v2 graph present. At least one terminal scenario. No refusal to proceed due to lack of data.

deterministic_checks.pattern: (?i)insufficient data|cannot model|need more data|unable to proceed
deterministic_checks.description: Skill must NOT refuse to proceed due to lack of numeric data — qualitative analysis is its core purpose

expectations.description: All four variables appear in the Variables table with a trend assignment, even though no data was provided
expectations.criteria: The Variables section lists Consumer Preference for Craft (or CPC), Number of Local Breweries (or NLB), Price Premium Tolerance (or PPT), and Mainstream Beer Sales (or MBS), each with an INC, DEC, or CONST trend value.

expectations.description: The negative relationship between craft preference and mainstream beer sales is captured as DEC(CPC, MBS) or equivalent
expectations.criteria: The matrix or relationship list reflects that craft preference and mainstream beer sales move in opposite directions — expressed as DEC notation or described as a negative/inverse relationship.

expectations.description: The Mermaid graph has at least two states and at least one transition between them
expectations.criteria: The stateDiagram-v2 block contains at least two distinct state declarations and at least one --> arrow connecting them.
